details.body: `src/cli/run.rs:2035-2059` still leaves a stale-state window after SIGTERM. The wrapper now calls `reconcile_signal_interruption()` only after the backend future finishes or after `RUN_STOP_GRACE_PERIOD` expires, but it does not persist the interrupted snapshot or remove `run.pid` when the signal is first observed. If a supervisor or a second signal kills the orchestrator during that wait, the project is still left in `Running` with a live `run.pid`, which is the durability gap this bead is meant to close. Persist the interrupted state (or an equivalent durable handoff marker) before entering the grace-period wait, then let later completion reconcile forward if the run actually finishes cleanly.

`src/cli/run.rs:2414-2434` makes stale backend cleanup unsafe under PID/PGID reuse. The old `is_backend_process_alive()` guard was removed so orphaned same-process-group children can be killed after the leader exits, but the replacement code now sends `kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` as long as the record contains authoritative identity fields, without first proving that numeric PID/PGID still belongs to the original backend leader. If that leader exited and the PID/PGID was reused, `run stop`/stale recovery can kill an unrelated process group or process. Add a reuse-safe validation strategy before signaling `-pid` (or refuse cleanup when that guarantee cannot be established).

`src/cli/run.rs:2524-2549` still leaves orphaned backend groups running in the exact case this bead is meant to fix. The new branch refuses cleanup whenever the recorded backend leader is gone, but Unix process groups remain signalable by PGID after the leader exits. That means a backend whose leader dies while descendants stay in the same process group is no longer killed by `run stop`; the new test in `tests/cli.rs:6667-6679` even locks in that the orphan stays alive. `kill_stale_backend_process_group` should still send `SIGKILL` to `-pgid` in this dead-leader case and only use direct-PID fallback when a live authoritative leader still exists.

`src/cli/run.rs:3357-3373` orders forced-stop cleanup incorrectly: `cleanup_backend_processes()?` runs before the orchestrator receives `SIGKILL`. Any backend cleanup error now aborts `run stop` before the SIGKILL branch executes, so a TERM-ignoring orchestrator can keep running after a failed stop attempt. The new refusal path at `src/cli/run.rs:2536-2549` makes this easy to trigger, and `tests/cli.rs:6678-6684` has to kill the orchestrator manually afterward. The command needs to guarantee the main orchestrator is killed first, then surface backend-cleanup failures separately or aggregate them after the main PID is definitely dead.

`src/cli/run.rs:2047-2069` and `src/cli/run.rs:2109-2140` still do not make SIGTERM cleanup durable. On signal, the wrapper now removes `run.pid`, but it only rewrites `run.json` as interrupted after the engine future returns or the grace timeout expires. If the process is killed by a second signal or supervisor escalation during that wait, the command never reaches `reconcile_signal_interruption_for_attempt`, so the project is left with `run.json` still at `Running` and no durable failed snapshot or `run_failed` journal event. Persist the interrupted snapshot (or an equivalent durable interrupted marker that resume/stop can trust) before entering the grace-period wait, then reconcile forward if the run actually finishes cleanly.

`src/cli/run.rs:2527-2557` and `src/cli/run.rs:2570-2580` make forced backend-group cleanup unsafe after the recorded leader exits. The new code sends `kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` and later enumerates `ps` by numeric `pgid == record.pid` even when `FileSystem::is_backend_process_alive(record)` is already false. Once the original group leader is gone, those numeric PID/PGID values can be reused by unrelated processes, so `run stop` / stale recovery can kill or wait on the wrong process group. Add a reuse-safe identity check for the live group leader (or refuse group cleanup once that guarantee is lost) before targeting `-pid` or matching `pgid == record.pid`.

Fix the SIGTERM handoff so it does not append a durable `run_failed` journal event before the engine future finishes. In `src/cli/run.rs:2098-2103` and `src/cli/run.rs:2146-2175`, the wrapper persists interruption state via `mark_running_run_interrupted()` and then still allows the engine future to continue. If the engine later reaches its normal failure path, it appends another terminal event using its stale in-memory `seq` counter at `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:5987-6000`, which can write a duplicate or out-of-order journal sequence and corrupt `journal.ndjson`.

Do not treat every dead tracked backend leader as a hard cleanup failure. `kill_stale_backend_process_group()` now errors immediately when `is_backend_process_alive(record)` is false (`src/cli/run.rs:2622-2626`), and stale recovery invokes that path before it can repair the snapshot (`src/cli/run.rs:2793-2795`). If a backend already exited cleanly but its `active-processes.json` entry survived a crash or forced stop, `run resume` on a stale Running snapshot fails even though there is nothing left to kill.

`src/cli/run.rs:2629-2650` still refuses to kill an orphaned backend process group after its leader exits. `kill_stale_backend_process_group()` enumerates surviving members, but if any remain it returns an error instead of signaling `-pgid`, and that refusal bubbles through cleanup into `run stop`/stale recovery (`src/cli/run.rs:2700-2724`, `src/cli/run.rs:2811-2813`, `src/cli/run.rs:3475-3483`). The new regression test at `tests/cli.rs:6580-6684` even asserts that the orphan stays alive. This leaves the forced-stop case from the bead unresolved: a TERM-ignoring orchestrator can be SIGKILLed while its backend orphan keeps running. The stop/recovery path still needs a safe way to target the recorded backend group, rather than codifying the leak.

`src/cli/run.rs:2632-2637` now rejects every tracked backend record whose `proc_start_*` fields are missing before it even checks whether that process is already gone. Because `RunBackendProcessRecord` deserializes those identity fields as optional (`src/adapters/fs.rs:177-187`) and `backend_process_is_authoritative()` only tests for their presence (`src/adapters/fs.rs:673-677`), a legacy or partially written `active-processes.json` entry with a dead PID now makes stale reconciliation and stop cleanup fail (`src/cli/run.rs:2811-2813`, `src/cli/run.rs:3475-3483`) even though there is nothing left to kill. The cleanup path should prune dead non-authoritative records and only refuse when such a record is still alive.

`src/cli/run.rs:2795-2817` runs `cleanup_stale_backend_process_groups()` before any snapshot/journal reconciliation, and the stale-recovery call sites at `src/cli/run.rs:3142-3155` and `src/cli/run.rs:3348-3376` propagate that error immediately. If backend metadata is corrupt or cleanup partially fails, `run stop`/`run resume` leave `run.json` stuck at `status = "running"` even though the orchestrator was already classified stale and the writer lease was reclaimed. I reproduced this with a stale run plus malformed `runtime/backend/active-processes.json`: `run stop` failed with the cleanup parse error and left `run.json` unchanged. The helper needs to persist the interrupted snapshot first and then surface backend-cleanup failures separately.

`src/cli/run.rs:3577-3603` acquires a fresh recovery writer lease before deciding whether the stopped run still needs stale reconciliation, and the reused liveness check at `src/cli/run.rs:1536-1597` treats any fresh CLI lease without an authoritative pid as `LegacyCliLease` instead of `Stale`. If the orchestrator removes `run.pid` during SIGTERM handling but exits before rewriting `run.json`, `run stop` now returns success with `Run for project '…' exited during stop with status 'running'.` and leaves the snapshot at `running`. I reproduced this with a TERM handler that deletes `run.pid` and exits: the command succeeded, `run.pid` was gone, and `run.json` still said `running`. The stop path should decide stale-vs-running before taking its own lease, or explicitly ignore the lease it just acquired when rechecking liveness.

`src/cli/run.rs:2020-2033` now persists the SIGTERM handoff by calling `mark_running_run_interrupted(...)` before the grace-period wait, and `mark_running_run_interrupted` removes `run.pid` immediately at `src/contexts/workflow_composition/engine.rs:821-843`. If the orchestrator then dies before the outer `CliWriterLeaseGuard` closes, the fresh CLI writer lease is left behind with no pid file. `run resume`/failed-state recovery cannot reclaim that lease, because the only fast-path reclaim check still requires a matching dead pid record (`src/cli/run.rs:1718-1753`, `src/cli/run.rs:1790-1854`). The project stays blocked until the 300s CLI lease TTL expires. Persist enough owner/pid identity to reclaim the dead lease, or release/convert the lease as part of the signal handoff instead of deleting the only reclaim evidence.

`src/cli/run.rs:2643-2678` still makes stale backend cleanup unsafe under PID/PGID reuse. When `backend_process_is_authoritative(record)` is true but `is_backend_process_alive(record)` is false, the code enumerates the current process group `pgid = record.pid` and sends `kill(-pid, SIGKILL)` anyway. A reused PID that became a new process-group leader will satisfy that numeric lookup even though it is unrelated to the recorded backend, so `run stop`/stale recovery can SIGKILL an arbitrary process group. Refuse group cleanup once the recorded leader identity no longer matches, or add a reuse-safe proof that the live PGID still belongs to the original backend leader before signaling `-pid`.

`src/cli/run.rs:2020-2033` intentionally persists only a failed snapshot during the SIGTERM handoff (`failure_class: None`), and the durable `run_failed` append is deferred to `finalize_signal_interruption_marker()` at `src/cli/run.rs:2036-2067` / `src/cli/run.rs:2150-2224`. If the process is SIGKILLed during that grace-period wait, the workspace is left with `run.json.status = failed` but no terminal `run_failed` event. That state is still treated as corrupt by milestone sync (`src/cli/run.rs:1264-1273`), so the handoff is not fully durable after an abrupt exit. Persist a journal-level handoff/terminal marker before waiting, or add a later repair path for failed snapshots missing `run_failed`.

`src/cli/run.rs:2198-2201` persists the new `cleanup_handoff`, but `src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:125-150` does that with an unsynchronized read-modify-write while the lease heartbeat is still running. `heartbeat_tick()` at `src/contexts/automation_runtime/cli_writer_lease.rs:620-625` also does a read-modify-write of the same lease file. If a heartbeat reads the old record just before the signal path writes `cleanup_handoff`, the heartbeat can write its stale copy back afterward and erase the handoff. That reintroduces the exact stale-lock problem this bead is fixing: `run.pid` is removed, the process dies, and recovery has no durable evidence to reclaim the fresh CLI lease until TTL expiry. The handoff write needs to be serialized with heartbeat updates (or merged atomically) instead of racing them.

The missing-`run_failed` repair only runs inside `sync_terminal_milestone_task_with_options()`, but that function returns immediately when `project_record.task_source` is absent (`src/cli/run.rs:1103-1105`). The signal path still persists only the failed snapshot with `failure_class: None` (`src/cli/run.rs:2070-2082`), and the later repair is reached only after the milestone/task-source gate (`src/cli/run.rs:1138`). For non-bead projects, a SIGKILL during the grace-period wait still leaves `run.json` in `failed (interrupted by termination signal)` with no durable `run_failed` event forever. The fix needs a project-wide repair/finalization path, not one limited to milestone-linked runs.

`RunBackendProcessRecord` still allows missing process-identity metadata (`src/adapters/fs.rs:177-187`), but `kill_stale_backend_process_group()` now returns early for every non-authoritative record (`src/cli/run.rs:2718-2730`). `cleanup_stale_backend_process_groups()` then unconditionally deletes the backend-process records for the attempt (`src/cli/run.rs:2829-2834`). That means a legacy or partially-populated record whose leader PID has already exited will no longer use `unix_process_group_members()`/`kill(-pgid)` to clean surviving descendants; the orphaned backend tree keeps running and the bookkeeping is removed so later recovery cannot target it. The new safety check should still allow dead-leader PGID cleanup when PID reuse has been ruled out, rather than dropping the record and abandoning the descendants.
